subject: Cardiology
title: Cardiomyopathy
preambleMarkdown:
# Cardiomyopathy

> **Clinical-review notice:** This is an examination-focused draft. Local protocols, medication choices, procedure thresholds, anticoagulation decisions, pregnancy/pediatric care, and device or surgical indications must be checked against current institutional and national guidance before clinical use.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Cardiomyopathy involves impaired ventricular filling, ejection, or myocardial performance causing congestion, low output, or both. Exam questions emphasize volume status, ejection fraction phenotype, precipitating causes, guideline-directed medical therapy, device indications, and acute decompensation sequencing.

sections.heading: Key Points
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Assess congestion and perfusion separately.
- Loop diuretics relieve congestion but do not replace disease-modifying therapy.
- HFrEF therapy centers on ARNI/ACEi/ARB, evidence beta blocker, mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist, and SGLT2 inhibitor when tolerated.
- Identify reversible causes such as ischemia, tachyarrhythmia, valve disease, toxins, thyroid disease, and myocarditis.
- Acute pulmonary edema or shock requires immediate stabilization.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Cardiomyopathy is a clinical syndrome of symptoms and signs caused by structural or functional cardiac abnormality, supported by objective evidence such as natriuretic peptides or imaging.

sections.heading: Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- HFrEF, HFmrEF, HFpEF, and improved EF.
- Acute decompensated versus chronic compensated disease.
- Left-sided, right-sided, or biventricular failure.
- Ischemic, hypertensive, valvular, genetic, infiltrative, inflammatory, toxic, tachycardia-mediated, or stress-related causes.

sections.heading: Epidemiology
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Heart failure is common in older adults and patients with hypertension, coronary disease, diabetes, kidney disease, obesity, valve disease, cardiomyopathy, atrial fibrillation, cancer therapy exposure, or prior myocarditis. Exams emphasize phenotype, congestion, perfusion, and reversible triggers.

sections.heading: Anatomy
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Key anatomy includes ventricular size and thickness, atrial size, valves, pericardium, coronary supply, pulmonary vasculature, and systemic venous drainage. Chamber geometry helps distinguish dilated, hypertrophic, restrictive, ischemic, valvular, and right-sided patterns.

sections.heading: Physiology
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Symptoms arise when filling pressures rise, forward output falls, or both. Neurohormonal activation initially preserves perfusion but later promotes sodium retention, vasoconstriction, remodeling, congestion, and arrhythmia risk.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Heart failure can result from systolic dysfunction, diastolic dysfunction, pressure overload, volume overload, ischemic scar, infiltrative disease, myocarditis, tachycardia-mediated dysfunction, toxin exposure, or stress cardiomyopathy. Decompensation often reflects infection, ischemia, arrhythmia, renal failure, medication nonadherence, or excess salt/fluid.

sections.heading: Etiology
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Major causes include ischemic heart disease, hypertension, valve disease, genetic cardiomyopathy, alcohol or stimulant exposure, anthracycline or other cardiotoxic therapy, myocarditis, infiltrative disease, endocrine disease, tachyarrhythmia, congenital disease, and peripartum cardiomyopathy.

sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Assess hypertension, coronary disease, diabetes, chronic kidney disease, obesity, sleep apnea, alcohol, cocaine or amphetamines, cardiotoxic chemotherapy, family cardiomyopathy, atrial fibrillation, valve disease, and prior myocarditis.

sections.heading: Genetics
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Genetic contribution is most important when presentation is early, familial, syndromic, associated with sudden death, or linked to cardiomyopathy, channelopathy, aortopathy, or congenital heart disease. First-degree relatives may need screening when an inherited syndrome is suspected.

sections.heading: Microbiology
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Microbiology is usually not the primary driver unless infection causes endocarditis, myocarditis, pericarditis, infected aneurysm, sepsis-related cardiovascular collapse, prosthetic-device infection, or vascular graft infection. Fever, bacteremia, immunosuppression, injection drug use, or recent procedures should prompt an infectious evaluation.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Dyspnea, orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, edema, fatigue, weight gain, rales, elevated JVP, S3, hepatomegaly, or cool extremities.
- Low-output states may present with confusion, renal dysfunction, hypotension, or narrow pulse pressure.

sections.heading: History
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Ask about onset, duration, triggers, exertional component, positional or pleuritic features, syncope, dyspnea, palpitations, chest pain, neurologic symptoms, bleeding, fever, medication use, stimulant use, pregnancy, recent procedures, prior ECG or imaging, prior cardiovascular disease, and family history of sudden death or inherited disease.

sections.heading: Physical Examination
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Assess vital signs, oxygenation, mental status, perfusion, volume status, pulses, jugular venous pressure, precordial impulse, heart sounds, murmurs, lungs, edema, neurologic deficits, skin findings, and signs of shock. Compare bilateral pulses and blood pressures when aortic or vascular disease is possible.

sections.heading: Red Flags
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Hypotension, altered mental status, cold extremities, or oliguria.
- Severe hypoxemia or pulmonary edema.
- New chest pain or ischemic ECG changes.
- Syncope or sustained ventricular arrhythmia.
- Rapidly progressive cardiomyopathy.

sections.heading: Differential Diagnosis
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Dyspnea and edema alternatives include COPD or asthma, pneumonia, pulmonary embolism, renal failure, cirrhosis, nephrotic syndrome, venous insufficiency, anemia, thyroid disease, obesity hypoventilation, and medication-related edema.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Approach
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Use ECG, chest imaging, natriuretic peptides, renal function, electrolytes, CBC, thyroid testing when indicated, and echocardiography.
- Evaluate ischemia and valve disease when clinically appropriate.
- Use cardiac MRI for myocarditis, infiltrative disease, scar, or uncertain cardiomyopathy.

sections.heading: Investigations
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Use ECG, chest imaging, natriuretic peptides, CBC, renal function, electrolytes, liver tests when congested, thyroid testing when indicated, troponin when ischemia is possible, echocardiography for EF and structure, ischemia evaluation, cardiac MRI for tissue characterization, and selected invasive hemodynamics.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Criteria
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Diagnosis should combine symptoms, examination, ECG or rhythm evidence, imaging, biomarkers, and accepted disease-specific criteria. Avoid diagnosing from one abnormal test in isolation when the clinical syndrome does not fit.

sections.heading: Severity Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Severity is based on hemodynamic stability, symptom burden, organ dysfunction, ECG or imaging risk features, biomarker pattern, ventricular function, lesion severity, arrhythmia risk, embolic risk, bleeding risk, frailty, and need for urgent procedural intervention.

sections.heading: Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
### Initial Management

- Assess airway, breathing, circulation, disability, and exposure. Place unstable patients on monitoring, obtain IV access, treat hypoxemia, correct immediately reversible causes, obtain an ECG, and escalate early to cardiology, emergency medicine, ICU, vascular surgery, electrophysiology, or cardiac surgery when high-risk features are present.

### Non-pharmacological Management

- Use education, smoking cessation, exercise or cardiac rehabilitation when appropriate, diet quality, weight management, sleep apnea treatment, alcohol or stimulant reduction, vaccination when relevant, and shared decision-making. Activity restrictions depend on acuity, symptoms, rhythm risk, structural disease, and procedure plans.

### Pharmacological Management

- Medication choices depend on the exact syndrome and contraindications. Common classes include antiplatelets, anticoagulants, statins, antihypertensives, beta blockers, calcium-channel blockers, nitrates, diuretics, guideline-directed heart-failure therapies, antiarrhythmics, antibiotics, or analgesics. Dose details require local protocol and patient-specific renal, hepatic, pregnancy, bleeding, and interaction review.

### Procedures

- Procedural options may include cardioversion, defibrillation, temporary or permanent pacing, catheter ablation, coronary angiography or PCI, CABG, valve intervention, vascular repair, thrombectomy, thrombolysis, pericardiocentesis, endovascular repair, or diagnostic catheterization depending on the topic.

### Surgical Management

- Surgery or structural intervention is considered for unstable anatomy, refractory symptoms despite medical therapy, high-risk coronary or valvular disease, congenital lesions, aortic disease, vascular complications, device infection, or complications such as mechanical rupture. Timing depends on acuity, anatomy, procedural risk, expected durability, and patient goals.

- Acute congestion: oxygen or ventilation support when needed, IV loop diuretics, vasodilators if appropriate, and trigger treatment.
- Shock: inotropes, vasopressors, mechanical circulatory support, and ICU consultation when indicated.
- Chronic care: guideline-directed therapy, sodium and fluid counseling when relevant, rehabilitation, device therapy, and close follow-up.
- Avoid medications that worsen HF when possible.

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Monitor symptoms, vital signs, rhythm, perfusion, urine output when ill, medication adverse effects, renal function and electrolytes when using diuretics or renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system therapy, anticoagulation safety, device function when present, and disease-specific imaging intervals.

sections.heading: Follow-up
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Follow-up should confirm diagnosis, review response to therapy, reconcile medications, address risk factors, arrange rehabilitation or specialist care, document warning symptoms, and ensure surveillance for recurrence, progression, procedural complications, or adverse drug effects.

sections.heading: Complications
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Complications may include shock, heart failure, pulmonary edema, myocardial infarction, stroke, systemic embolism, sudden cardiac death, malignant arrhythmia, bleeding from therapy, renal injury, infection, recurrent hospitalization, procedure-related injury, and chronic functional limitation.

sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Prognosis depends on acuity, ventricular function, comorbid disease, age, kidney function, adherence, response to therapy, reversibility of the cause, and access to timely emergency or procedural care. Early recognition and appropriate follow-up reduce preventable morbidity.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Prevention includes smoking cessation, blood-pressure control, lipid management, diabetes care, physical activity, diet quality, weight management, treatment of sleep apnea, avoidance of cardiotoxic exposures, vaccination when relevant, and family screening for inherited disease when indicated.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Explain the diagnosis, uncertainty that remains, warning symptoms, medication purpose and adverse effects, follow-up plan, activity guidance, and when to seek urgent care. Use plain language and confirm understanding.

sections.heading: Nursing Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Trend vital signs, oxygenation, pain, mental status, perfusion, rhythm, intake/output when ill, and response to treatment.
- Escalate promptly for red flags, new neurologic symptoms, recurrent chest pain, hypotension, syncope, worsening dyspnea, or malignant rhythm.
- Reinforce medication adherence, follow-up, lifestyle changes, and procedure instructions.

sections.heading: Pharmacy Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Check allergies, renal and hepatic function, pregnancy status when relevant, interactions, duplicate anticoagulant or antiplatelet therapy, QT-prolonging drugs, and adherence barriers.
- High-risk medications require indication, duration, monitoring plan, and bleeding or toxicity counseling.
- Reconcile medications at transitions of care.

sections.heading: Dentistry Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Most cardiology conditions do not automatically require antibiotic prophylaxis for dental care. Consider endocarditis prophylaxis only for guideline-defined high-risk cardiac conditions and procedures, and coordinate anticoagulant management for invasive dental procedures when needed.

sections.heading: Special Populations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
### Pediatrics

- Pediatric presentations may reflect congenital disease, inherited arrhythmia, myocarditis, Kawasaki disease, multisystem inflammatory syndromes, or rare familial vascular disease. Use age-specific vital signs, doses, and pediatric specialist pathways.

### Pregnancy

- Pregnancy changes heart rate, plasma volume, coagulation, and imaging or medication choices. Maternal stabilization comes first in emergencies. Coordinate cardiology, obstetrics, anesthesia, and neonatology for significant disease.

### Older Adults

- Older adults often have atypical symptoms, polypharmacy, frailty, kidney disease, cognitive impairment, and higher bleeding or procedural risk. Goals of care and functional status should shape decisions.

### Renal Impairment

- Renal disease changes biomarker interpretation, medication dosing, contrast risk, electrolyte vulnerability, and bleeding risk. Avoid delaying life-saving imaging or intervention when the emergency risk is greater than contrast risk.

### Hepatic Impairment

- Assess coagulopathy, thrombocytopenia, drug metabolism, volume status, and procedural bleeding risk. Medication selection and anticoagulation decisions require individualized review.

### Immunocompromised Patients

- Maintain a lower threshold for infection, atypical presentations, drug interactions, and device-related complications. Coordinate antimicrobial and procedural decisions with specialists when infection is possible.

sections.heading: Guideline Comparison
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Major North American and European guidance generally agrees on stabilizing emergencies first, using ECG and echocardiography appropriately, treating modifiable risk factors aggressively, and matching procedures to anatomy and risk. Differences commonly involve thresholds, timing, scoring systems, medication preferences, and local resource pathways. Use current local guidance for patient care.

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stability determines the first action.
- Compare current ECG, imaging, and symptoms with prior data whenever possible.
- Treat the patient, not a single number.
- Reassess after each intervention.
- Imported notes below are provenance; current synthesis should be reviewed against up-to-date guidelines.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Delaying emergency treatment for nonessential testing.
- Ignoring medication contraindications, renal function, pregnancy, or bleeding risk.
- Assuming a normal initial test excludes a dynamic cardiovascular process.
- Failing to arrange follow-up after an ED or inpatient diagnosis.
- Removing imported source notes instead of preserving provenance.

sections.heading: Exam Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
### USMLE Step 1

- Focus on anatomy, physiology, pathology, pharmacology, ECG mechanisms, hemodynamics, and classic disease associations.

### USMLE Step 2 CK

- Prioritize presentation, next best diagnostic test, initial stabilization, red flags, and first-line management.

### USMLE Step 3

- Emphasize sequencing: stabilize first, choose the right unit of care, monitor treatment response, prevent complications, and plan discharge follow-up.

### ABIM

- Expect guideline nuance, comorbid disease, renal impairment, anticoagulation decisions, procedural indications, and treatment failure.

### ABFM

- Focus on prevention, screening when applicable, risk-factor control, medication adherence, counseling, and recognizing emergencies that need transfer.

### Surgery boards

- Know when vascular, cardiac, or structural intervention is urgent; understand anatomy, perioperative risk, and complications.

sections.heading: High-Yield Facts
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Warm/wet, warm/dry, cold/wet, and cold/dry profiles guide acute management.
- Do not start or up-titrate beta blockers during cardiogenic shock.
- SGLT2 inhibitors are part of modern HF therapy across several EF ranges.
- ICD and CRT decisions depend on EF, QRS, rhythm, symptoms, and optimized therapy.

sections.heading: Evidence Gaps
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Areas requiring ongoing review include individualized thresholds for intervention, risk prediction in underrepresented groups, long-term comparative effectiveness of procedures and devices, optimal antithrombotic duration, and implementation of guideline-directed therapy in patients with multimorbidity.

sections.heading: References
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- ACC/AHA/ACEP/NAEMSP/SCAI. 2025 Guideline for the Management of Patients With Acute Coronary Syndromes. https://professional.heart.org/en/science-news/2025-guideline-for-the-management-of-patients-with-acute-coronary-syndromes
- AHA/ACC/HFSA. 2022 Guideline for the Management of Heart Failure. https://professional.heart.org/en/science-news/2022-guideline-for-the-management-of-heart-failure
- ACC/AHA/ACCP/HRS. 2023 Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Atrial Fibrillation. https://www.ahajournals.org/doi/10.1161/CIR.0000000000001193
- ACC/AHA. 2020 Guideline for the Management of Patients With Valvular Heart Disease. https://professional.heart.org/en/science-news/2020-acc-aha-guideline-for-the-management-of-patients-with-valvular-heart-disease
- ACC/AHA. 2022 Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Aortic Disease. https://professional.heart.org/en/science-news/2022-guideline-for-the-diagnosis-and-management-of-aortic-disease
- ACC/AHA. 2024 Guideline for the Management of Lower Extremity Peripheral Artery Disease. https://professional.heart.org/-/media/PHD-Files-2/Science-News/2/2024/2024-PAD-guideline-slide-set.pdf

### Source Excerpt: Primary Myocardial Diseases

_Source lines: 5475-5843._

```text
IX. PRIMARY MYOCARDIAL DISEASES
A. Myocarditis

## 1. Discussion

a. Definition. Myocarditis is an inflammatory process of the myocardium. Patho-
logically, myocarditis is characterized by focal inflammatory infiltration. The
process frequently extends to the pericardium, causing a pericarditis.

b. Causes
i. Infectious causes
a) Viral causes include infection with coxsackie B virus, echovirus,
HIV, influenza B, parainfluenza, Epstein-Barr virus, and hepatitis B
virus.

b) Bacterial causes include Lyme disease and infection with Corynebac-
terium diphtheriae, Neisseria meningitidis, Mycoplasma, # -hemolytic
streptococci, and Staphylococcus aureus.

c) Other infectious causes include Chagas disease and toxoplasmosis.

ii. Immunologic causes include rheumatic fever, giant cell myocarditis,
and Kawasaki syndrome.

iii. Toxic causes include cyclic antidepressants, hydrochlorothiazide, vac-
cines, cocaine, and radiation.

2. Clinical features
a. Symptoms
i. Constitutional symptoms such as fever, fatigue, myalgias, and headache
often accompany cardiac-specific symptoms.

ii. Chest pain, often vague or pleuritic and accompanied by palpitations, is
a common complaint.

a) The pain may represent associated pericarditis.

b) Myocardial irritability, conduction system involvement, or both are
common and may be perceived as palpitations.

iii. Dyspnea may be present in more severe cases in which myocardial dys-
function has progressed to heart failure.

b. Physical examination findings may be normal in patients with mild disease.

In patients with more moderate illness, a muffled S1 along with an S3 and a
mitral regurgitation murmur may be elicited. A pericardial friction rub may
be heard with associated pericarditis. Severe disease results in progressive
systolic dysfunction and typical signs of CHF.

3. Differential diagnoses
a. Chest pain must be differentiated from that of acute MI or ischemia and
other causes of chest pain as discussed in section I.C.

b. Palpitations must be differentiated from benign palpitations and other
arrhythmias as discussed in section IV.

c. Dyspnea can be secondary to many primary pulmonary etiologies
(e.g., pneumonia, pneumothorax, pulmonary embolism, obstructive
lung disease). Patients with acute CHF, valvular disease, or pulmonary
hypertension might also present with complaints of dyspnea.

## 4. Evaluation

a. Electrocardiography. An ECG most commonly reveals nonspecific ST-
segment and T-wave changes. Elevations of the ST segments, due to accom-
panying pericarditis, along with conduction disturbances (e.g., AV block,
prolonged QRS duration) reflect more severe disease. Arrhythmias, ectopy,
or both may be noted.

b. Radiography. A chest radiograph is not helpful unless the illness has pro-
gressed to heart failure, when pulmonary vascular congestion and cardio-
megaly may be noted.

66 	 	S T E P - U P T O E M E R G E N C Y M E D I C I N E

Cardiovascular Emergencies
c. Laboratory studies reveal elevations of the WBC count and sedimentation
rate as well as elevations in cardiac enzyme levels.

d. Myocardial biopsy is diagnostic and may be indicated after initial emer-
gency management to direct continued therapy.

5. Therapy. Emergent treatment is supportive and symptom directed. Antibiotics
should be administered if an acute bacterial cause is suspected. Steroids remain
controversial, limited to the treatment of idiopathic disease, and are not part of
the emergency management of myocarditis.

## 6. Disposition

a. Patients with evidence of more severe disease accompanied by myocardial
dysfunction, CHF, or significant arrhythmias, or those suspected of having
bacterial myocarditis, should be admitted to a monitored setting.

b. Most patients with idiopathic or viral myocarditis can be safely treated and
followed as outpatients. Discussion of follow-up plans with a cardiologist or
the patient's primary care physician is appropriate.

B. Cardiomyopathies are the third most common form of cardiac disease in the
United States, after ischemic and hypertensive heart disease.

## 1. Introduction

a. Definition. This group of cardiac illnesses is characterized by primary
abnormalities or dysfunction of the heart muscle.

b. Cause. By definition, the cause of true cardiomyopathy is unknown; how-
ever, viral infections may account for a subgroup of illnesses currently
thought to be idiopathic. Contrary to this strict definition, however, the
term "cardiomyopathy" is used to refer to a number of disorders of cardiac
function (Table 2-7).

2. Dilated (congestive) cardiomyopathy
a. Discussion. Dilated cardiomyopathy is the most common type.

i. Causes (see Table 2-7). Alcohol is the most common cause of dilated
cardiomyopathy in the United States.

ii. Pathogenesis. Systolic and diastolic dysfunction due to increased ven-
tricular volume and pressure eventually leads to decreased cardiac
output and overt heart failure. Cardiomegaly with chamber enlargement
can result in arrhythmias and mural thrombi. Patients typically have a
progressively downhill course; death usually occurs within 2 years of
symptom onset unless cardiac transplantation is attempted.

b. Clinical features
i. History. The patient should be questioned regarding a recent pregnancy,
substance abuse, a recent viral illness, and systemic illness.

ii. Symptoms suggestive of left- and/or right-sided heart failure predominate.

Chest pain, often vague, may be present. Palpitations are common. Cere-
bral or systemic embolic phenomena secondary to mural thrombi occur
with complaints of focal weakness, numbness, or a cold, painful extremity.

iii. Physical examination findings
a) Evidence of cardiac enlargement with an enlarged point of maximal
impulse, an audible S3 and S4 , and murmurs of mitral or tricuspid
regurgitation (or both) are often present.

b) Findings of left- and right-sided heart failure may be evident.

c) Focal neurologic findings or a cold, pulseless extremity may be
found when embolic phenomena occur.

c. Differential diagnosis. The differential diagnosis includes ischemic or
hypertensive heart disease, acute ischemia or infarct, decompensation of
valvular disease, and other causes of pulmonary edema.

d. Evaluation
i. Radiography. A chest radiograph reveals cardiomegaly with variable
degrees of pulmonary congestion.

ii. Electrocardiography. An ECG most commonly shows nonspecific
changes and may reveal sinus tachycardia, atrial fibrillation, AV con-
duction abnormalities, or ventricular arrhythmias.

C A R D I O V A S C U L A R E M E R G E N C I E S 	 	67

Cardiovascular Emergencies
iii. Bedside echocardiography will reveal chamber enlargement and
decreased ventricular function.

e. Therapy
i. CHF should be treated as discussed in III.E.2.

ii. Anticoagulation should be initiated if there are no contraindications.

iii. Antiarrhythmic therapy may be necessary (see section IV). Rapid atrial
fibrillation can usually be controlled with intravenous diltiazem, digoxin,
or both. Implantation of automatic defibrillators or surgical ablation of
arrhythmic circuits is gaining favor for treatment of rhythm disturbances.

iv. Nonemergent, longer term treatment includes discontinuing any offend-
ing agents (e.g., alcohol, drugs). Maximizing known disease-specific
therapies, such as nutritional supplementation or therapies for infiltrative
malignancies, is essential. Cardiac transplantation may be a consideration.

f. Disposition depends on the severity of symptoms, but most patients present
to the ED in a decompensated state and require admission.

3. Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy
a. Discussion. Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy is characterized by muscular
hypertrophy of a nondilated left ventricle. Most patients have regional
Classification of Cardiomyopathies	TABLE 2-7
Dilated 	Hypertrophic 	Restrictive
Idiopathic 	Asymmetric septal hypertrophy 	Endomyocardial fibrosis
Secondary 	Friedreich ataxia 	Loffler endocarditis
Infective 	Secondary
Viral myocarditis 	Infiltrative/granulomatous
Protozoal 	Familial storage diseases
Spirochetal 	Amyloidosis
Bacterial 	Progressive systemic sclerosis
Fungal 	Fibroelastosis
Toxic 	Radiation
Ethanol
Cocaine
Heavy metals
Radiation
Drugs
Doxorubicin
Nutritional/metabolic
Collagen vascular diseases
Infiltrative/granulomatous
Amyloidosis
Sarcoidosis
Malignancy
Hemochromatosis
Peripartum heart disease
Neuromuscular disorders
Familial storage diseases
68 	 	S T E P - U P T O E M E R G E N C Y M E D I C I N E

Cardiovascular Emergencies
variations in the extent of hypertrophy, but the majority demonstrate dis-
proportionate septal hypertrophy.

i. Hypertrophy of the left ventricle results from cardiac muscle cell disor-
ganization and variable myocardial fibrosis. Diastolic dysfunction occurs
secondary to stiffness of the hypertrophied muscle, which leads to in-
creased end-diastolic pressures and restricted ventricular filling.

ii. Outflow obstruction due to systolic motion of the anterior leaflet of
the mitral valve has been demonstrated in up to 25% of patients and
is dynamic in nature depending on the end-diastolic volume, the heart
rate, and the afterload.

b. Clinical features
i. Symptoms vary depending on the extent of the disease and the patient's
age and are related to the progressive diastolic dysfunction, not the pres-
ence or degree of outflow obstruction.

a) Sudden death may be the first clinical manifestation and is more com-
mon in children or young adults. Syncope and near-syncope can occur.

b) Dyspnea on exertion is a common complaint, and an exercise history
must be obtained.

c) Anginal chest pain is due to the increased oxygen requirements of
the hypertrophied muscle that are not met by coronary blood flow.

d) Palpitations due to arrhythmia may be a complaint. Atrial arrhyth-
mia is usually poorly tolerated due to the loss of atrial contribution
to ventricular filling.

ii. Physical examination findings are often benign and unrevealing.

a) Examination may reveal an audible S4 as well as a double or triple
atrial impulse palpable over the chest wall.

b) Cardiac examination reveals the hallmark systolic murmur, which
is harsh, occurs after the S1 , and is diamond-shaped in only 25% of
patients. The murmur is best heard at the left sternal border and may
be accentuated by performing a Valsalva maneuver. Passively raising
the legs, squatting, or a sustained handgrip will decrease the inten-
sity of the murmur. A blowing quality to the murmur may be heard
at the apex and represents mitral regurgitation due to the poor appo-
sition of the anterior mitral valve leaflet.

c. Differential diagnoses
i. Differential diagnoses include aortic or mitral valvular disease, hy-
pertensive heart disease, ischemic heart disease, and other causes of
diminished diastolic compliance such as restrictive cardiomyopathy or
constrictive pericarditis.

ii. Athlete's heart syndrome, in which ventricular hypertrophy is physi-
ologic, is often accompanied by similar physical examination findings,
ECG abnormalities, and benign arrhythmias, and may be confused with
hypertrophic cardiomyopathy. However, a history consistent with that of
an asymptomatic trained athlete is a clue to this diagnosis.

d. Evaluation. The diagnosis of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy may be enter-
tained in a patient with a history of exertional dyspnea, classic physical
examination findings, and a family history of the disease; however, this
diagnosis is not usually made based on the ED evaluation.

i. Radiography. A chest radiograph is initially normal but may show a
mild to moderate increase in the cardiac silhouette. Pulmonary vascular
congestion is unusual.

ii. Electrocardiography. An ECG demonstrates left ventricular hypertro-
phy and left atrial enlargement. Deep Q waves (septal Q waves) in mul-
tiple leads may mimic those that indicate an old MI (see Figure 2-10).

Atrial and ventricular rhythm disturbances are common.

iii. Bedside echocardiography is the diagnostic modality of choice and is
useful for distinguishing athlete's heart syndrome from hypertrophic
cardiomyopathy.

C A R D I O V A S C U L A R E M E R G E N C I E S 	 	69

Cardiovascular Emergencies
e. Therapy
i. Emergent management of the acutely symptomatic patient, especially
one with chest pain, is with # blockers to decrease the force of ven-
tricular contraction and increase diastolic filling time. Calcium chan-
nel blockers may be of benefit in decreasing ventricular wall stiffness,
thereby decreasing ventricular filling pressures. Amiodarone is effective
in reducing supraventricular as well as ventricular arrhythmias. Di-
goxin, nitrates, diuretics, and # agonists should be avoided.

ii. Longer term therapy may include implantation of a dual-chamber pace-
maker and surgical myomectomy.

iii. Endocarditis prophylaxis is indicated as discussed in XI.F.2.

f. Disposition. Patients who present with syncope or chest pain require
admission to a monitored setting for further evaluation and treatment.

Patients in whom the diagnosis is suspected but who do not demonstrate
acute signs and symptoms should be advised to avoid strenuous activity
until more thorough evaluation can be performed by a cardiologist.

4. Restrictive cardiomyopathies
a. Discussion. Restrictive cardiomyopathies are also characterized by diastolic
dysfunction, which results from noncompliance of the ventricles secondary
to myocardial fibrosis, infiltration, scarring, or thrombus. The progressively
rigid ventricle leads to impedance of filling, high filling pressures, and per-
sistently elevated venous pressure.

b. Clinical features
i. Symptoms relate to the persistently elevated venous pressures from a
small, rigid ventricular cavity and include those of right- and left-sided
heart failure.

ii. Physical examination findings may include jugular venous distention,
often with Kussmaul sign, findings of right-sided heart failure, and find-
ings of CHF. Heart sounds may be distant, and an S3 and an S4 are com-
mon. A murmur of mitral regurgitation is also common.

c. Differential diagnoses include constrictive pericarditis, cardiac tamponade,
pulmonary hypertension, causes of right-sided heart failure (e.g., right ven-
tricular infarct, valvular dysfunction), and causes of left-sided heart failure.

F I G U R E
2-10 	ECG in a patient with hypertrophic cardiomyopathy.

70 	 	S T E P - U P T O E M E R G E N C Y M E D I C I N E

Cardiovascular Emergencies
d. Evaluation
i. Radiography. A chest radiograph may be unremarkable but may show evi-
dence of pulmonary vascular congestion with a normal cardiac silhouette.

ii. Electrocardiography. An ECG usually reveals nonspecific changes and
may show low QRS voltages, also a finding in constrictive pericarditis.

iii. Echocardiography typically reveals symmetric thickening of the ven-
tricular walls with normal or mildly reduced systolic function.

e. Therapy. Emergent treatment is symptom-directed and entails the judicious
use of diuretics, vasodilators, and antiarrhythmics as necessary. Patients
with amyloidosis may be sensitive to digoxin; therefore, this medication
should be used with caution.

f. Disposition. Depends on the severity of the presenting symptoms, but most
patients will require admission for treatment of heart failure.
```
